caption:
Out of a £6.80 pint, pubs only keep around 28p here in the UK.

VAT is an absolute killer for the leisure and hospitality sector.

latestComments.text: Keep up the good work!!!
Insane, as a long time Barman that’s scary
Same for a gym x
It’s really bad . It’s our biggest concern.

It’s the same in the hairdressing industry. We’ve been vat registered for day one and it’s been workable (just ) but with the other escalating increases it’s just impossible to make a living owning a business in the hospitality and service industry 😢
Well put and agreed - it was us in the telegraph article!
Same issue amongst our communities. We are a hairdressers and coffee shop with a huge VAT bill due to our biggest cost being labour.

@dominicbrit bold assumption that I pay my team peanuts — I never have and never will.

I’m not blaming any one political party either. I’m fairly apolitical — I just care about good long-term policy, which, frankly, all sides seem to struggle with.

Small businesses aren’t faceless entities — they’re usually individuals and families who’ve taken real risk to build something. When pressures increase, both businesses and people feel it at the same time.

Also not sure why Brexit was brought into this — it’s not really relevant to the point I was making. For what it’s worth, I voted remain.

Feels like you’ve made a few assumptions there — if that’s coming from bad experiences, I genuinely hope things improve 👍

caption: Why are people not spending, why are business owners pessimistic about the future? What does it mean for entrepreneurs? Whats your thoughts?
firstComment: Nice hoody james! I'm missing running camps this year due to work commitments:(

latestComments.text: Nice hoody james! I'm missing running camps this year due to work commitments:(
Thats all most recessions are, a feeling.. apart from 2008 that was different.
We're B2B and a couple of our customers are spending big time.  Unfortunately they make parts for warships so mixed feelings about that one...
Every aspect of business or personal life is taxed to the hilt … and very little by way of incentives to encourage entrepreneurs …

There’s nowhere to go “shopping” anymore.  I used to live for arriving at a destination first thing in the morning, spending the entire day mooching about a vast mix of both independents and chains, ending up having to extend the parking and staying for dinner.  Without the risk of getting robbed!  Now there’s empty high streets.  Violent shopping malls.  And I’ve not walked the streets of Oxford/Regent street since 2020 when it turned into a walking dead free for all.  Im hoping markets start thriving again because people want that in person experience, but the hospitality venues that pull people to the areas can’t afford to stay open, nor offer affordable produce.  Then there’s a definite influx of a change of consumer taste buds in all sectors.  There’s a lot of people out there more than happy with cheap S£!t from Ch!/*……… whether it be clothes, accessories, home wear even electricals from internet sales and being satisfied with sloppy food outlets.
The negative thought cycle has always been a British thing - always see the worst in things. Which is why it’s so easy to win in the UK.
Love Dan Kennedy. I attended his event many years ago. Thank you @jamessinclairentrepreneur
For any hospitality business the gap between risk and reward has shrunk to nothing! Any business owner is now working a lot harder for less money

I’m just getting started again after a prior business failure and bankruptcy during lockdown, spent 6 years working on myself and am now more ready than ever, it’s in the eye of the beholder, and if some think it’s a bad time for business then they’re right. Love your blogs and podcasts James 👏 very inspiring

caption:
A big frustration for me in the UK is that we are a secret...

We need someone to shake this country and go, go, go.

We need to stop the politics of envy.

We need to keep millionaires in the country.

We need low tax, low regulation, and a stronger entrepreneurial economy that everyone will benefit from.

Check out the full podcast with my good friend @dodge.woodall on YouTube “Essex Boy to £40 Million Empire & 2,000 Staff: James Sinclair”

caption:
James Sinclair admits that when it comes to work and life balance, he’s probably a workaholic. His mind simply doesn’t switch off.

For him, entrepreneurship is like an extreme sport. It’s the game he loves to play - constantly thinking, solving problems and building things. 

When you’re running dozens of businesses at once, there’s always something happening in your head.
But he believes things like podcasts, speaking and creating content actually help sharpen that thinking.

That’s why sharing knowledge with other business owners - whether on stage or through content - is such an important part of what he does.

caption:
Self service, AI and robots are going to become very common in the hospitality sector very soon.

When around 40% of your turnover goes on wages, people do not want to work weekends, and many simply do not want to work in the sector at all, it is hard to see how things can carry on as they are.

What do you think?
firstComment: Never will we have robots!!! We will close first

latestComments.text: Never will we have robots!!! We will close first
V sad
At the same point I don’t want a screen shoved in my face for a fine dining venue… it has to fit in

Tech has a time and a place…. It should enhance the experience and empower staff to focus on providing great hospitality. Handheld pos and payment devices are a good example of letting staff cover a greater area of the venue and having more time for proper interactions with customers.
I actively avoid places that try and make me order via a screen. Its called hospitality. Screen culture couldn't be less hospitable
👏👏👏 100% right

Let's not mistake technology for progress or value though. Its simply one metric, one lever you can pull. What you might gain in efficiency you could lose in long term intangible value, culture and human connection, which in hospitality and leisure are so important and so defining for an establishment.

replies.text: @lukeamaartin1 But people do not want to do the job. M
